
Make sure to add `using System.Text.Json;` to both files if not already present.

## 4. ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs — IL2026/IL3050

File: `/tmp/sv-e4b863182912408ca9ee08a90c7e9003/before/Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs`

The method `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` calls `RegisterClientFactory` which is marked with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]`. Since this is calling into an external interface method we can't change, we need to propagate the attributes.

Add these attributes to the method:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Binding strongly typed objects to configuration values is not supported with trimming. Use the Configuration Binder Source Generator (EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator=true) instead.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Binding strongly typed objects to configuration values requires generating dynamic code at runtime, for example instantiating generic types. Use the Configuration Binder Source Generator (EnableConfigurationBindingGenerator=true) instead.")]
```

The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`. Add both attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method (the second overload, around line 27-31).

## 5. GenericOperationSource.cs — IL2087

File: `/tmp/sv-e4b863182912408ca9ee08a90c7e9003/before/GenericOperationSource.cs`

The generic type parameter `T` on the class `GenericOperationSource<T>` is used with `typeof(T)` in reflection calls. Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]` to the type parameter.

Change:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<T> : IOperationSource<T>
```
to:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T> : IOperationSource<T>
```

## 6. ArmOperation.cs — IL2087/IL2090

File: `/tmp/sv-e4b863182912408ca9ee08a90c7e9003/before/ArmOperation.cs`

The method `IsResource<T>()` uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]` to the type parameter of that method.

Change:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

Make sure `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` is present in the file.

## 7. ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs — IL2026/IL3050

File: `/tmp/sv-e4b863182912408ca9ee08a90c7e9003/before/Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs`

Line 31: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);`
The `ManagedServiceIdentityType` struct has a custom `JsonConverter` (`ManagedServiceIdentityTypeConverter`). Since the converter is internal and defined in the project, we can call it directly instead of going through JsonSerializer.

Replace line 31:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
new ManagedServiceIdentityType.ManagedServiceIdentityTypeConverter().Write(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);
```

Wait, that writes a full JSON object. Looking at the converter's Write method, it writes `"type": value`. That's what Serialize does too. But we need to avoid JsonSerializer. Actually let me reconsider.

The converter's `Write` method does:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type");
writer.WriteStringValue(model.ToString());
```

So we can just inline that logic:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

Line 49: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions);`
Here `item.Value` is a `UserAssignedIdentity`. Since it implements `IJsonModel<UserAssignedIdentity>`:
```csharp
((IJsonModel<UserAssignedIdentity>)item.Value).Write(writer, ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions);
```

Line 206: `type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");`
The converter's `Read` method parses a JSON object `{"type": "value"}` and returns the ManagedServiceIdentityType. The `property` here is from `element.EnumerateObject()` and is a `JsonProperty` with name "type". So `$"{{{property}}}"` creates a JSON string like `{"type":"SystemAssigned"}`.

We can inline the converter's logic. Looking at the converter, it finds a property value and calls the constructor. Since `ManagedServiceIdentityType` is a struct with a string constructor, we can just do:
```csharp
type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());
```

Line 218: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText())`
Replace with: `UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value)`

Please make all these edits. Use `view` to confirm exact content before editing.

data.content: I need to fix 3 remaining issues. Let me look at the problematic files:
data.reasoningText:
OK, so issues:
1. `ResponseError` from Azure.Core does NOT implement `IJsonModel<ResponseError>` or `IPersistableModel<ResponseError>`. Need a different approach.
2. `ArmOperation.Rehydrate<T>` and `RehydrateAsync<T>` need `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]` on their `T` parameter too (cascading from `IsResource<T>` and `GenericOperationSource<T>`).
3. `ResourcesArmOperation.cs` and `ResourcesArmOperationOfT.cs` have the same `ToObjectFromJson` issue as the ManagementGroups ones.


4. For ResponseError, I'll register it in the source-generated context with `[JsonSerializable(typeof(ResponseError))]` and use the context for serialization and deserialization instead.

data.content: Now let me fix all remaining issues. `ResponseError` doesn't implement `IJsonModel<T>`, so I need to use a source-generated context. Let me add it to the SourceGenerationContext and use that:

data.reasoningText:
Ah, `ResponseError` from Azure.Core has its own `JsonConverterAttribute` but the converter doesn't have a parameterless constructor. This means the source generator can't handle it. We can't source-generate for `ResponseError`.

Since `ResponseError` doesn't implement `IJsonModel<T>`, `IPersistableModel<T>`, and can't be source-generated, but it does have a `JsonConverter` registered via attribute, the safest approach is to just use `JsonSerializer` directly but with `JsonSerializerOptions` that already know about the converter. The original code was `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error)` and `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(rawText)` — these work because the `JsonConverter` attribute on `ResponseError` is picked up by the default options.


For AOT compatibility, I need to sidestep the reflection-based metadata resolution. I could manually register the converter in custom `JsonSerializerOptions`, or since `ResponseError.Converter` exists but isn't accessible parameterlessly for the source generator, I could handle serialization manually by writing the JSON element directly and parsing the text for deserialization.

Looking at the `ResponseError` type from Azure.Core more closely — if it has a `[JsonConverter]` attribute with a parameterless constructor, the source generator should work. Otherwise, I could create a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` wrapper that I control and register in the source-gen context.

The most practical path forward is to handle serialization and deserialization manually using `JsonElement` — either by converting to raw JSON and writing it directly, or parsing the JSON back into the object. Though each approach still seems to hit the same reflection issue.

Actually, I'm realizing the real solution might be simpler: just revert `ResponseError` back to using the standard `JsonSerializer.Serialize` and `Deserialize` calls without source generation, and find a different way to handle the warning. The `[JsonConverter]` attribute on `ResponseError` means the runtime will find and use the converter automatically, even if the source generator can't see the parameterless constructor. The proper fix would be marking the method with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`, but that would cascade the warning everywhere these methods are called.

Let me think about this differently — instead of relying on the source generator, I could create a custom `JsonSerializerOptions` instance with the converter pre-configured and pass it explicitly to the serialization calls. That way we're not triggering the reflection-based metadata resolution path. Or even simpler, I could write a minimal hand-coded serializer for `ResponseError` since we know exactly what fields need to be serialized. the simplest approach is to use `GetRawText()` and `WriteRawValue()` to pass through the JSON directly, which avoids reflection entirely. For deserialization, I can construct from the raw JSON using `JsonDocument`. But when serializing, I still need to write the error property to the `Utf8JsonWriter` — using `WriteRawValue()` with `SerializeToUtf8Bytes()` still hits reflection. Let me try using `JsonSerializer` with explicit `JsonSerializerOptions` that includes a custom converter instead, which should sidestep the generated metadata approach.

Writing custom converter... The real solution here is to write a custom JsonConverter that manually handles the serialization and deserialization of ResponseError using Utf8JsonReader and Utf8JsonWriter, then register it through JsonSourceGenerationOptions. Since ResponseError typically just has `code` and `message` properties, I can examine how it's actually used in this codebase to understand its structure and implement the converter accordingly. Writing the converter... Looking at the auto-generated serialization code, these files handle wire format serialization using `JsonSerializer.Serialize()`. The real issue is that `ResponseError` has a `[JsonConverter]` attribute, so the converter exists at runtime, but the AOT analyzer can't verify this statically and throws warnings. The most practical solution would be to remove `ResponseError` from the problematic serialization path entirely, since the converter should handle it automatically when called through the standard serialization pipeline. a custom `JsonConverter<ResponseError>` to handle serialization properly, since I don't know the exact structure of the ResponseError type and shouldn't guess. Alternatively, I could use `JsonSerializerOptions` created from the source generation context with reflection as a fallback for unknown types, which would let me serialize the Error object without triggering trimming warnings.

Writing the converter class... I need to check what properties are actually available on the ResponseError type from the package to make sure I'm serializing everything correctly.

data.content: `ResponseInnerError` is internal. Let me take a different approach — simplify the converter to handle just the accessible properties and use `JsonMetadataServices` to create the type info directly:
data.reasoningText:
Two issues:
1. `ResponseInnerError` is not accessible (internal type in Azure.Core)
2. The source generator still can't generate metadata for `ResponseError` because of its internal converter

For issue 1, I'll simplify the converter to not use `ResponseInnerError` — instead just write/read the inner error as raw JSON using the simpler constructor.

For issue 2, the SYSLIB1030 warning means the source generator didn't produce a `ResponseError` property on the context. So my approach of using `SourceGenerationContext.Default.ResponseError` won't work even with the custom converter in the options. 

The source gen options `Converters` attribute isn't overriding the inaccessible converter on the type itself. I'll switch strategies entirely — instead of relying on the source-gen context for `ResponseError`, I'll create a dedicated `JsonSerializerOptions` instance with my custom converter registered and use the regular `JsonSerializer.Serialize/Deserialize` overloads that accept options.

Setting up serialization options...

But even this approach triggers IL2026/IL3050 warnings because the non-generic overloads still require type resolution at runtime. The generic version `JsonSerializer.Serialize<ResponseError>(writer, Error, options)` with the converter should theoretically work, but it still warns. The real solution for AOT-safe serialization with a custom converter is to manually create a `JsonTypeInfo<ResponseError>` — in .NET 9 I can use `JsonTypeInfo.CreateJsonTypeInfo<T>` and register the converter there. Actually, the simplest approach that will definitely work is to skip source generation entirely.

Creating JsonTypeInfo manually...

data.content: Now for `ResponseError`, since it's an external type with an inaccessible converter and internal nested types, the cleanest approach is to just write/read the raw JSON directly using `JsonElement`:

data.reasoningText:
For ResponseError, I'll take the simplest approach that's AOT-safe. Since ResponseError has `Code` and `Message` public properties and a constructor `ResponseError(string code, string message)`, I'll:

1. For serialize: write the JSON manually using `writer.WriteStartObject(); writer.WriteString("code", Error.Code); writer.WriteString("message", Error.Message); writer.WriteEndObject();`
2. For deserialize: parse the element and construct using the 2-param constructor.

BUT - wait. I also need to handle `Details` and `Target` properties. Let me check what's accessible...


The full constructor signature takes more parameters including `target`, `JsonElement`, `ResponseInnerError`, and `details`, but since `ResponseInnerError` is inaccessible and I can't construct it, I'll stick with the simpler 2-param constructor. For serialization, since the ResponseError object came from Azure.Core's own deserialization, I can extract and write out the properties directly.

The real issue is that ResponseError has its own `[JsonConverter]` attribute, so at runtime it serializes fine — the IL2026 warning is just the trimmer complaining it can't verify this statically. Given the inaccessible converter and internal types, wrapping the serialize/deserialize logic in methods marked with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` would work but creates cascading warnings up the call chain.
